content: @iclr_conf I do not feel this leak is a disaster. For example, some journals publish reviewer names publicly. If the reviewers can write, why are they afraid of their name being seen? Is science still open?

@iclr_conf Let's do not blame anyone, esp. the organizing committee, and do not worry, but think about how to make it better, but keep in mind that nothing will be perfect. In any case, science will move forward, but apparently with bumps.
@iclr_conf sad to see this
@iclr_conf Don’t submit in Iclr anymore.
@iclr_conf But the bigger problem is that now reviewers cannot edit their scores and comments. Another bug or on purpose?

@iclr_conf I suggest that the ICLR committee learn about zero-knowledge proofs. This cryptographic primitive is already widely used in the cryptocurrency industry to provide verifiable computation while preserving privacy.
@iclr_conf Care to respond to my question about getting back to the AC of a withdrawn submission? Tagged you here and sent emails. No response.

@iclr_conf 😱I believe it is the PC’s responsibility to inform all reviewers, ACs, and SACs by email, not just through social media, about the situation. Not everyone is present on these platforms, and the situation is quite serious. It is also important to receive clear guidance.#ICLR2026

@iclr_conf The leak severely undermines academic fairness. The loss of reviewer anonymity discourages critical feedback. Authors gain potential for off-the-record lobbying, violating equitable debate. The credibility of all acceptance decisions is fundamentally questioned.

@iclr_conf JUST A QUESTION

How should you deal with a bad actor who impersonates an author to contact reviewers, aiming to get the real author reported to ICLR 2026 and disqualified to reduce competition?

@iclr_conf Additionally, some social media platforms have already exposed cases where malicious reviewers significantly increased scores after being exposed. Would it be fairer to revert scores to their original values for evaluation, while having the AC provide a meta-review for scoring?

@iclr_conf Leaking reviewer and author information can lead to significant anomalous score increases, which is completely unfair to those manuscripts that face malicious reviews or reviewers who have no interaction throughout the process. How does the organizer plan to address this issue?
@iclr_conf The information has been downloaded as an .jsonl file and spreaded all over the places

@iclr_conf How about embracing the leak, to analyze it, adapt it, expose irresponsible reviwers and unethical authors, and reform the peer review process. About 10000 submissions already leaked, which cannot be undone. How about deanonymize all the submissions’ authors and reviewers.
@iclr_conf Could you please clarify why it is not possible for reviewers to edit their reviews (and scores) anymore now on OR and if this is going to be fixed soon?

@iclr_conf You assigned some incompetent reviewers who don’t even understand the basic common sense of the field. I’ve been educating them to comprehend my paper, and two out of the four have raised their scores. Yet you’ve now closed the score revision channel.
@iclr_conf just for fun😄 https://t.co/6NMdlAEPiC

I’m starting to feel that the reviewer assignment for my paper was completely unfair. None of my three reviewers have responded — not a single word — even though my rebuttal has been sitting there for more than ten days. It’s incredibly frustrating. How is this supposed to be a fair review process?

@iclr_conf Reverting reviews to the start of discussion is unreasonable.
1) It unfairly erases legitimate score increases made SECURELY before the leak (Nov 27).
2) Relying solely on ACs for this volume is unrealistic & negates the rebuttal process.

Fix: Rollback to Nov 27, not Day 1.
@iclr_conf Could you please keep all comments and just revert score changing after that day?

@iclr_conf Double blindness is necessary to protect faculty from the wrath of established powerhouses. However, I believe in a central tenet. Review with grace and the conviction that you would defend your review in front of a committee. Then you will not have any problems with privacy.

@iclr_conf reverting the score back to day 1 is really really unreasonable and unnecessary. It is so unfair to those who  took the discussion seriously and got the score raised. Please please reconsider the decision. Retain the comments and revert the score back to Nov 26. Please.
@iclr_conf I hope that the efforts of reviewers and authors will be treated fairly and not be let down

@iclr_conf There should be guidance immediately about what authors are allowed to include in new comments. Most people's instinct will be to summarize (or indeed, exaggerate) positive discussions and score increases for the AC's benefit. But allowing this defeats the point of re-anonymizing
@iclr_conf putting the "open" in openreview fr fr
@iclr_conf It’s your fault, but you are punishing researchers?

This is a serious breach, and people have every right to be frustrated. Anonymity is the core of fair peer review, but the tone of this statement feels less like accountability and more like warnings and penalties.

For a leak this big, the community needs transparency, clear guidance, and concrete next steps. Thousands of authors and reviewers are affected, and trust in the process is shaken. Focusing on punishments instead of explaining how fairness will be restored doesn’t help.

@iclr_conf My question now is, what if the reviewers never acknowledged the rebuttal in the first place, and they were already convinced by the response from the authors, what is going to happen? Or authors should deal with the luck fate brings?
@iclr_conf Are you removing the comments from the reviewer also? What if there is multiround discussion between reviewer and authors?
@iclr_conf Please ask openreview to turn off public review. There are some guys randomly deanonymize reviewer in comments.

@iclr_conf Why not set the review stage to private (not publicly accessible) like ICML and NeurIPS? Now someone is deliberately using a script to disclose the information of reviewers who gave a score of 2 for each paper, this is a malicious act of sabotage.
@iclr_conf It seems that reviewers cannot edit comments or scores right now. What's the next-up plan?

@iclr_conf If someone did a few brief tests, immediately emailed the PCs to report it, did not store/share any leaked identities, and did not contact any participant, could this still lead to a desk rejection?

@iclr_conf I learned that in some public comments, the real accounts of anonymous reviewers have been disclosed. To prevent such breaches of anonymity, I recommend temporarily disabling the public commenting feature.

@iclr_conf I've seen a report on social media by an ICLR reviewer stating they have been doxxed via a public comment on OpenReview. Maybe at this point it would be good to not allow any more public comments to ICLR reviews.
@iclr_conf Why keep the comments and discussions? It’s reverting nothing actually
@francoisfleuret What now?

open models sound noble in manifesto form, but they systematically favor the already-powerful, incentivize performative niceness or performative cruelty, and turn scientific evaluation into a hybrid of Reddit and gladiatorial combat. 

Double-blind review is flawed and leaky, but replacing it with fully open identities doesn’t fix the problems..it exacerbates most of them while creating elegant new ones. The middle ground (e.g., signed but non-public reviews to editors, or optional open discussion only after acceptance) is almost always superior to the utopian extremes.

@prfsanjeevarora @ylecun LeCun's full proposal enforces identity verification via ORCID integration, preventing this specific leak vector. The shift to mandatory open review, however, increases signal-to-noise ratio but risks career retaliation, requiring robust platform-level pseudonymity controls.
@prfsanjeevarora @ylecun Exciting potential for change! Let's embrace innovation and transparency in the review process.
@prfsanjeevarora @ylecun Such a pivotal moment in AI research. Time to revisit @ylecun's visionary proposal.

@prfsanjeevarora @ylecun This is a systems problem as much as a community one. When the trust layer is this fragile, it's a sign the whole architecture needs a rethink. LeCun's proposal feels more relevant than ever.

@prfsanjeevarora @ylecun it's not that it is highly biased, it's that it's exploiting workers and their resources 

you cannot ignore this

One potential way for OpenReview to earn income while staying neutral is to host class-project-level “venues” that allow students to peer-review each other’s work.

I once TAed an undergrad capstone class with a required paper writing component. Instead of having 2-3 graders read 80× 10+ page essays, I wrote a small algo to distribute and collect feedback via email, and I’d say the feedback was much more detailed and diversified than any grader could produce. 

Being able to produce and engage with criticism is one of the most essential skills, and the rebuttal process is one of the best real-world scenarios to practice this craft, where no platform has done it better than OpenReview.

I’ve already seen some course-level, project-style venues on OpenReview, but maybe it’s time for them to push for institution-level subscriptions (like Canvas).
@gan_chuang I think this accident could be very beneficial in the long run. Now those irresponsible reviewers would think more carefully before posting reviews.

Andrew's dedication underscores the longevity of open academic networks. Supporting such teams is vital to preserving open science.

It might mean we do away with the conference specific review process. Papers get reviewed through a central system with efficient timelines like TMLR. Reviews are public and on regular cycles the best papers get selected to present at the next meeting. Best part about conferences has always been interacting with people and this keeps that part while maintaining review quality. Current conference system is indeed noisy and I am not sure the rebuttal period resulting in authors and reviewers doing 10 different things all at once, responding and commenting, not to mention the extra research/experiments that is done in such a compressed timeframe, is resulting in better quality. The ICLR leak is a disaster, but let’s talk about how to actually save the Peer Review process.

The Fix: Ending anonymity for irresponsible reviewers.

ICLR maintains high submission quality (at least in early years) because rejected papers are public—authors fear the reputation hit. Why don't we hold reviewers to the same standard?

If a review is fundamentally irresponsible (after confirming by ICLR Program Committee), the reviewer should lose the privilege of anonymity. 
• Good review = Anonymous (if desired). 
• "Rejected" review = Public Name.

Clearly a pivotal moment where we need to rethink peer review. Some thoughts:

1. I have been an advocate of fully non-anonymous peer review. On one hand, people will be careful about what they write but on the other hand, most people will just not sign up for reviewing.
2. Conferences have badly failed at scaling peer review. This has been an issue at least since 2019 where I remember AAAI had close to 9k submissions. It's not possible to have 24k papers submitted per major conference and have them well reviewed. 
3. At this point the review signal is extremely noisy and what determines acceptance is whether you can "trick" 3 people into following your narrative. Submit a decent paper enough times and it will get accepted. 
4. There are way too many niches and too many papers coming out daily. I don't think anyone is up to date anymore. Therefore anyone reviewing your papers is likely not in a position to make a good judgement.
5. The competition due to artificially low acceptance rates is so intense that it's not unnatural for a reviewer to see any paper they are reviewing as a threat. This combined with point 4 means that the reviews will either be shallow or adversarial, both useless.
6. Clearly AI reviews are here to stay so best if we invest in proper infra and training so that people use AI ethically and appropriately and thus help repair peer review.

@youjiaxuan Don't you think this will result in some reviewers being afraid to be critical and recommend rejection of a paper by fear of repercussions?

Way too nuclear imo. Deanonymization is equal, if not even harsher, than desk rejection. Yet desk rejection is typically limited to <<50 papers each cycle. 

It doesn’t change things at scale because, for a punishment this harsh, you need concrete, indisputable evidence of something objectively severe (e.g., not posting the review at all). Broad “unresponsible” behavior is hard to define or agree upon if you want impact at scale, and the few PCs are not going to be helpful in determining this.

I do think the best way is to introduce a CREDIT SYSTEM where we earn points by doing good. Say +1 for completing one regular review, +3 for being outstanding, +5 for handling emergency review requests, etc. Then such points can be spent to redeem perks like free registration, or to invite additional reviewers in muddy situations, etc. We can even use points as a refundable “submission fee” to discourage unready work and flawed recycles — like if you work is so trash your submission fee is forfited. If not, we give it back even if the work is not accepted.

Then, reviewers on the same paper + AC can vote and impose a fine if they all agree a certain reviewer is “unreasonably unresponsive” or whatever. There can also be plenty of automated fines (didn’t post review on time, didn’t reply, etc.). 

I feel like we need a finer, more flexible enforcement granularity than desk rejection, not a harsher one.
@youjiaxuan Both the reviewers and ACs

this is such a mistake it’s crazy. the reason double blind exists is to protect reviewers from malicious authors when providing critical feedback, and protecting authors from malicious reviewers.

I respectfully think revealing authors of rejected work is a very different kind of game than revealing ACs/reviewers. IMO the ICLR rejection-lives-forever model doesn’t get much pushback because most work ends up on arXiv anyway, so there’s no point in hiding names; and everyone knows ML conferences are kinda random, where having a few rejections is the norm.

But the backdrop is totally different when it comes to revealing ACs/reviewers. A classic edge case is: what if you (fairly) reject a paper from some pitiful giant? That can create a lot of unnecessary friction. But this is another topic for another day.

Conference mechanism-wise, I do agree what I proposed can be complicated. Maybe we can ease people in by rolling things out gradually — like for the first few conferences, we just do:

* +1 point per review.
* +3 for being outstanding.
* +5 per emergency review.
* -10 per author per submission, refundable if passes the a certain low bar.
* -5/day after the deadline until the review is posted.

More complicated maneuvers can be introduced later. It’s not perfect and might need a lot more careful thinking, but it sure feels better than what we have now.

IMO, penalizing reviewers by deanonymization may discourage people from writing critical reviews. Another way can be to build a reviewer credit system to encourage more responsible reviews.

Reviewers can have a credit profile that includes behavior scores and their past review statistics. This can be provided to AC/SAC when making the decision for a paper. Reviews from reviewers with a better record should have higher weight.

We can also consider reviewer credit when we assign reviewers to papers to reduce reviewing variance.

If you want your reviews to count more later, you need to produce high-quality reviews to maintain the credit.

Couldn't agree more on the need for structural fixes. It seems unfair for the PC to bear the brunt of responsibilities that might really fall to the OpenReview platform and technical chairs. We clearly need better tools and more dedicated engineers to maintain the system at today's scale.

I do think the best way improve review experience is to introduce a CREDIT SYSTEM where we earn points by doing good. Say +1 for completing one regular review, +3 for being outstanding, +5 for handling emergency review requests, etc. Then such points can be spent to redeem perks like free registration, or to invite additional reviewers in muddy situations, etc. 

We can even charge points as a refundable “submission fee” to discourage unready work and flawed recycles — like if you work is so trash your submission fee is forfited. If not, we give it back even if the work is not accepted. More smart ideas can be adopted to achieve things like incentivizing researchers to serve as reviewers even when they are not authoring  — with the correct implementation, it should change up the reviewer-load situation quite a bit...

Then on the penalty end, maybe reviewers on the same paper + AC can vote and impose a fine if they all agree a certain reviewer is “unreasonably bad” or whatever. There can also be plenty of automated fines (didn’t post review on time, didn’t reply, etc.). 

I wonder, from a PC’s perspective, do you think such system would be worthy of trying? I like it because it seems to bring incentive and accountability, but I am not sure if it actually makes sense from the eyes of the pros (and there are so few prior art on this to consult).
